hypothesis: no-op self-test
cache casted RMSNorm weight to avoid per-call dtype copies
enable built-in torch.compile
enable torch.compile (re-judged: argmax+JS gate)
enable torch.compile (Pareto gate, argmax+JS)
torch.compile for large inputs only (rows>=8192)
torch.compile large-only; regression=confidently-slower gate

SoftmaxScalingMLP.forward runs per scaled-attention call (ICL layers, dist embedder, decoder). It currently issues 3 full-size [B,S,H,D] elementwise kernels: (1+tanh) add, base_scales*modulation mul, q*scales mul. Reassociate the algebraically-equal expression q*base_scales*(1+tanh) into a single mul (q*base_scales) followed by a fused torch.addcmul(a, a, tanh), removing one elementwise kernel launch per call. On this launch-bound model (GPU busy 6-57%), fewer kernel launches reduce overhead. Pure fp reordering (distribution of the multiply), which the referee's gate tolerates; argmax/JS stay within run-to-run noise.

Remove redundant per-call .contiguous() copies of q/k/v in the shared SDPA helper that every TabPFNV3 attention routes through. These tensors are permute(0,2,1,3) views of (B,S,H,D)-contiguous projection outputs, so the head (last) dim already has stride 1 — the only contiguity PyTorch's flash/efficient SDPA backends require. The .contiguous() therefore forced a full memory copy (the #1 GPU op: aten::copy_, 7.974ms, and ~3 large copies per flash call) without changing backend selection or math. Removing it eliminates ~498 large copies per predict in the launch/copy-bound regime. Output is bit-identical: flash is still selected (last-dim stride 1 satisfied) and the kernel math is independent of batch/head/seq strides. Batch slicing for the >65k-grid workaround keeps last-dim stride 1. Expected mechanism: fewer copy kernels + bytes moved → faster, especially on copy-dominated small inputs.

In FeatureDistributionEmbedder, each InducedSelfAttentionBlock copies its input (transpose(1,2).contiguous(), line 1264) AND its output (transpose(1,2).contiguous(), line 1286). Between consecutive layers these are inverse operations: the output is (B,R,C,E)-contiguous, and the next block transposes it back to (B,C,R,E) and re-copies. Returning a view instead (drop the output .contiguous()) means the next block's transpose(1,2) recovers the already-contiguous (B,C,R,E) storage, so its .contiguous() returns self (no kernel/copy). out_BCRE is contiguous in all paths (Linear/out-proj output reshaped, return_hidden tensor, or the in-place chunked buffer), so the recovered transpose is genuinely contiguous. Net: eliminates ~2 large aten::copy_ per layer boundary (copy is the #1 GPU op, launch/copy-bound regime). Bit-exact: only the physical layout of the handoff tensor changes; cat/Linear downstream accept strided inputs. Expected: fewer copy kernels + bytes moved on every dist-embed layer, helping both deterministic (typical) and small (sample) points without any fp reordering.

_DtypeMatchingRMSNorm.forward calls self.weight.to(input.dtype) on every invocation (~398/predict), each a kernel launch + small copy. In inference the weight is frozen, so the casted result is identical every call. Cache the casted weight keyed on the source weight identity, its ._version, and the target dtype; reuse it when unchanged. This removes ~398 redundant cast/copy kernel launches per predict in the launch-bound eager (typical) regime while producing a bit-identical casted tensor (same .to() math, same F.rms_norm call). The cache path is skipped under torch.compiler.is_compiling() so the compiled large-input (sample) graph is unchanged (no python-side-effect graph break). Expected: fewer launches → faster typical, neutral sample.

apply_rope's final combine `t*cos + t_rotated*sin` issues two separate elementwise `mul` kernels plus an `add` kernel on the full (...,S,D) tensor, and it runs twice (q and k) per ColumnAggregator TransformerBlock across all blocks. Replacing it with `torch.addcmul(t*cos, t_rotated, sin)` fuses the second multiply and the add into a single kernel, eliminating one full-size elementwise launch per RoPE call. On this launch/elementwise-bound small model (aten::mul is 6.8ms/1181 calls, aten::add 3.1ms; GPU only 6-57% busy), fewer kernel launches reduce overhead. This is pure fp reassociation (distribution/ordering of a multiply-add), which the referee's argmax+JS gate tolerates; outputs stay within run-to-run noise. Applies to both interleaved and non-interleaved branches via the shared return.

SoftmaxScalingMLP.forward issues three pointwise kernels per scaled-attention call: `1 + tanh(...)` (add), `base_scales * modulation` (mul), and `q * scales` (mul). It runs on every CrossAttentionBlock-with-scaling (dist embedder), every ICL block, and the decoder. Replacing `base_scales * (1 + tanh)` with `torch.addcmul(base_scales, base_scales, tanh)` fuses the add and one multiply into a single kernel (base_scales + base_scales*tanh == base_scales*(1+tanh)), eliminating one full-size elementwise launch per call in this launch/elementwise-bound regime (aten::mul 6.8ms/1181, aten::add 3.1ms; GPU 6-57% busy). Unlike iteration 4's RoPE addcmul (which mixed bf16 t_rotated with fp32 sin and hit a slow fallback), here base_scales, the query-MLP output, and tanh all share q_BSHD's dtype, so addcmul stays on its fused same-dtype path. This is pure fp redistribution of a multiply-add (distribution, not reassociation — mathematically identical), which the argmax+JS gate tolerates; outputs stay within noise. Expected mechanism: fewer kernel launches → faster typical (eager, launch-bound) and neutral sample.

apply_rope recomputes cos/sin (arange + outer-mul + cos + sin + 2×cat ≈ 6 small kernels) on every call. A single RotaryEmbedding instance is shared across all ColumnAggregator blocks (self.rope), and within each Attention layer it's invoked for both q and k, so the SAME cos/sin (depending only on seq_len, device, frozen inv_freq, interleaved) are regenerated 2×num_layers times per forward. Memoize them in a per-function dict keyed on (id(inv_freq), inv_freq._version, seq_len, device, interleaved); reuse when unchanged. This collapses all those redundant cos/sin builds to one per distinct (seq_len) per predict, cutting many small kernel launches in the launch-bound eager (typical) regime. Bit-exact: cached cos/sin are computed by the identical ops and used read-only in the identical `t*cos + t_rotated*sin` arithmetic (only t_rotated, which depends on t, stays per-call). The cache is skipped under torch.compiler.is_compiling() (the prior reason it wasn't cached), so the compiled large/sample graph is unchanged with no python-side-effect graph break. Expected: fewer launches → faster typical, neutral sample.

SoftmaxScalingMLP.forward recomputes `base_scales = base_mlp(_safe_log_seqlen(n))` on every scaled-attention call, but base_scales depends ONLY on (n, dtype, device) and the frozen inference weights -- never on the query. That tiny chain (~8 small kernels: ones/mul/clamp/log/cast + two Linears + GELU + view) runs per ICL block, per dist-embedder cross-attn block (per col/row chunk), per decoder call, and the same modules are invoked with the same n across the harness's repeated timed predicts. Caching base_scales per (n,dtype,device) on the module removes all those redundant tiny launches from steady-state timed runs in this launch-bound regime (GPU 6-57% busy). Output-preserving and bit-exact: the cached tensor is exactly what base_mlp would recompute (deterministic, frozen weights); the per-query path (query_mlp/tanh/mul) is unchanged. Caching is skipped under torch.compiler.is_compiling() (compiled graph identical) and during training (weights mutate). Distinct from the prior rejected SoftmaxScaling edits, which only reassociated the elementwise muls; this eliminates kernel launches instead.

_safe_log_seqlen runs on every scaled-attention call (each ICL block, each dist-embedder cross-attn block, and the decoder). Its eager path materialises log(n) through ~5 tiny GPU kernels (torch.ones -> mul -> clamp -> log -> to). When `n` is a concrete Python int (eager, non-compiled = the `typical` operating point), I compute log(max(n,1)) on the host and create the 0-d result in one torch.full call, eliminating ~4 tiny kernel launches per scaled-attention call in this launch-bound regime (GPU 6-57% busy). The symbolic SymInt path is preserved exactly under torch.compiler.is_compiling(), so the compiled `sample` graph is byte-identical (no new guards, no recompiles) and stays neutral. Output-preserving: same clamped log(n) cast to the same dtype; only the host-vs-device evaluation of a scalar log differs (fp reordering the gate tolerates), so argmax/JS stay within noise.

In ManyClassDecoder.forward, one_hot_targets_BNHT = unsqueeze(2).expand(...,H,...).contiguous() materializes a full copy of the large (B, N, H, T) one-hot value tensor. It is then passed straight into _chunked_class_attention, which does F.pad / reshape / permute / reshape().contiguous() — each of which already produces a fresh contiguous tensor from the broadcast view. The explicit .contiguous() is therefore a redundant second materialization of a large tensor (aten::copy_ is the #1 GPU op). Dropping it lets the broadcast view flow into the existing pad/reshape, which copies it exactly once. Output is bit-identical (reshape/pad of the broadcast view yields the same data, just one fewer copy). Expected mechanism: one fewer large copy kernel + bytes moved per predict in the copy/launch-bound multiclass decoder.

In ICLAttention's non-cached path, x_train = x_BRE[:, :N] is a row-strided (non-contiguous) view when N<R. It feeds two nn.Linear calls (k_projection, v_projection); each F.linear flattens to 2D, forcing an implicit aten::copy_ of the strided slice — i.e. it's materialized twice per ICL layer. Calling .contiguous() once makes both subsequent projections' reshapes free, eliminating one large copy per ICL layer in the copy/launch-bound regime. Output is bit-identical (contiguous changes layout only; no-op when N==R). Expected mechanism: fewer aten::copy_ kernels + bytes moved across all ICL layers (this is the hot path for both typical and sample operating points, which route heavily through ICL attention).

Remove the redundant per-block `.contiguous()` on the InducedSelfAttentionBlock output (B,R,C,E). The returned tensor is always re-materialized by its consumer — the next dist-embedder block immediately does `x_BRCE.transpose(1,2).contiguous()`, and the final block's output is consumed by ColumnAggregator's `torch.cat((cls, x), dim=2)`, which copies into a fresh contiguous buffer regardless. So the trailing `.contiguous()` is a redundant large copy (aten::copy_ is the #1 GPU op at 7.97ms/1886 calls). Dropping it removes one full-size copy kernel per dist-embedder block per row-chunk in the launch/copy-bound eager (typical) path, while the compiled (sample) path is neutral (inductor already fuses contiguity into the downstream op, and large-row sample is GPU-bound). Output-preserving: only contiguity differs; all values identical (same proven pattern as the accepted SDPA-`.contiguous()` removal).

In Attention.forward (the self-attention used by ColumnAggregator's TransformerBlocks), q/k/v are computed as three separate nn.Linear GEMMs on the SAME input x_BSE with identical output dims. Concatenating the three (bias-free) weight matrices into one and issuing a single F.linear + split is mathematically/bit-identical to three independent matmuls (it's just a block-structured GEMM), but issues 1 GEMM kernel instead of 3 per call, reducing launches in the GEMM-heavy region (aten::mm 3.98ms/800 + aten::addmm 1.86ms). The concatenated weight is frozen at inference, so it is cached on the module (keyed on the source weight identity + ._version) to avoid rebuilding it each call; under torch.compiler.is_compiling() it is rebuilt inline so the compiled (sample) graph stays a pure function with no python-side-effect graph break. The q/k/v split slices the contiguous last dim, then unflatten/reshape into (B,S,H,D) keeps last-dim stride 1 (flash-eligible) and the downstream RoPE/SDPA math is unchanged. Expected: fewer GEMM launches → faster typical (eager) and neutral-or-faster sample.

SoftmaxScalingMLP.forward recomputes `base_scales = base_mlp(_safe_log_seqlen(n))` on every scaled-attention call, but base_scales depends ONLY on (n, dtype, device) and the frozen inference weights — never on the query. That tiny chain (log + two Linears + GELU + view ≈ 8 launch-overhead-bound kernels) runs per ICL block, per dist-embedder cross-attn block, and the decoder, with the SAME n across the harness's repeated timed predicts. Caching it bit-exactly eliminates those launches in the launch-bound eager (typical) regime. This is materially different from the previously-rejected always-on base_scales cache: that version regressed `sample` (0.9971, confidently slower) because the large-input path's ICL blocks run EAGER with large n and paid the per-call dict-lookup cost. Here the cache is GATED to small n (< 8192, mirroring the compile size-gate) using a single-slot tuple (cheaper than a dict), so the large/sample path takes the no-cache branch (just one int compare) and stays neutral, while typical (small n) gets the full ~6% win. Bit-exact: the cached tensor is exactly what base_mlp recomputes (deterministic, frozen weights); the per-query path (query_mlp/tanh/mul) is unchanged. Cache is skipped under torch.compiler.is_compiling() and training, so no compiled graph is perturbed. Expected: typical clearly >3% faster, sample neutral.

RMSNorm is the launch storm: torch<2.12's eager F.rms_norm decomposes into ~5 separate kernels (pow/mean/add/rsqrt/mul). The profiler's aten::mean x400, aten::pow x403, aten::rsqrt x398 counts match ~398 RMSNorm calls/predict — i.e. ~1600+ tiny kernel launches purely from normalization in the launch-bound (GPU 25-60% busy) regime. Fuse them by compiling F.rms_norm ONCE with dynamic=True (so the varying batch/row dims don't recompile) into a single Inductor triton kernel, reused across every _DtypeMatchingRMSNorm instance. This collapses ~5 launches → 1 per norm call. Gated to `not self.training and not torch.compiler.is_compiling()`: inside the existing sample-path compiled subgraphs the outer graph already fuses (is_compiling True → plain F.rms_norm, unchanged), while the eager ICL-loop/output norms (run eager even on the sample path) and the entire typical path get the fused kernel. Output-preserving: same rms-norm math, only op-fusion/fp-reordering that the gate already tolerates for the compiled sample path. Distinct compile variants are bounded (2 normalized_shapes × weight-presence), well under cache_size_limit.

Stage-3 ICL transformer blocks run eager even on the sample (>=8192 rows) path where torch.compile is already enabled and proven (+8.6% from compiling stages 0-2). Each ICLTransformerBlock issues the RMSNorm launch-storm (pow/mean/add/rsqrt/mul x N), Linear projections, softmax-scaling pointwise, and MLP as dozens of tiny eager kernels per layer per predict — pure launch overhead in this 25-60%-GPU-busy regime. Extend the existing compile coverage to the ICL loop: compile each block once (keyed on module id, since all blocks share the same forward code object but have distinct weights — reusing _compiled's __func__ key would wrongly alias them) with dynamic=True and mark_dynamic on the batch/row dims so varying test-row counts don't recompile. num_train stays a python-int guard (fixed per operating point) so the train-key slice point is static. This collapses the per-block launch storm into fused Inductor kernels, the same mechanism that already sped up stages 0-2. Strictly scoped to enable_torch_compile (set False for <8192 rows at line 1749), so the eager 'typical' path is byte-identical and only the already-compile-tolerant 'sample' path is touched (fp-reordering within the gate's tolerance). force_recompute and KV-cache/return_kv paths are left untouched. _compiled_module cache is popped in __getstate__ for picklability like the existing cache.

Fused QKV/KV projections to cut GEMM launches in the launch-bound eager attention path. Each attention module (Attention, CrossAttention, ICLAttention) currently issues separate nn.Linear matmuls for projections that share the SAME input tensor: Attention projects q,k,v all from x_BSE (3 matmuls); CrossAttention projects k,v both from the key/value input (2 matmuls); ICLAttention projects k,v both from x_train (2 matmuls). Concatenating those weights along the output dim and doing ONE F.linear, then slicing the result, is mathematically identical (each output row is the same dot product) and removes 1-2 aten::mm/addmm launches per attention call. These modules run eager — the dist-embedder's CrossAttention (InducedSelfAttentionBlock x2/layer x column-chunks) and column-aggregator's self-Attention dominate the eager 'typical' path, and the ICL blocks run eager on both paths — so this directly attacks the launch-overhead (GPU 25-60% busy). The concatenated weight is cached on the module (keyed on each weight's _version/dtype/device, like the accepted base_scales cache), so torch.cat runs once and is reused across all timed predicts. The fused path is disabled under torch.compiler.is_compiling() and training, so the compiled stages-0-2 'sample' subgraphs stay byte-identical (no graph break, no recompile) and only eager regions get fewer launches. ICLAttention skips fusion when return_kv (cache-build) so the KV-cache entries keep owning their own contiguous storage (no memory-retention behaviour change). Output-preserving: only the GEMM tiling/fp-reordering may differ, which the argmax+JS gate tolerates.

aten::copy_ is the #1 GPU op (7.97ms/1886 calls) in this launch-bound regime. The dist-embedder stacks N InducedSelfAttentionBlock layers; each block's forward does `transpose(1,2).contiguous()` on entry (BRCE->BCRE) and again on exit (BCRE->BRCE). Between consecutive layers these two large layout copies are exact inverses that cancel — pure wasted bandwidth+launches. Chain the blocks directly in the flattened (B*C, R, E) layout so the round-trip collapses to ONE transpose+contiguous at entry and ONE at exit, eliminating 2*(num_layers-1) big copies per dist-embedder pass. Bit-identical: transpose+contiguous is a pure layout copy that never changes values, and the per-block induced-attention math operates on the same logical (B*C,R,E) values regardless of how the buffer was produced. Strictly gated to the eager inference fast path (not training, not compiling, no KV cache / hidden / recompute), so the compiled `sample` subgraph (_process_row_chunk) is byte-identical — only the eager `typical` path gets fewer copies. forward_flat reuses the existing chunked_evaluate_maybe_inplace + _induced_attention exactly. Expected: fewer large copy_ kernels + bytes moved -> faster typical, neutral sample.

Dist-embedder cross_attn_block1's query is always the frozen inducing_vectors (unsqueeze+expand → shares storage), so layernorm_q(ind)+q_projection(ind) is data-independent and bit-identical every call. In the eager 'typical' path this block runs per dist-embedder-block × per column-chunk × per predict, each time relaunching the RMSNorm storm + a GEMM purely to recompute a constant query. Add a `query_is_constant` flag to CrossAttentionBlock that caches the projected query q (B,Q,H,D) keyed on (inducing storage ptr, shape, dtype) and passes it to CrossAttention via a new optional `precomputed_q`, skipping layernorm_q + q_projection on hits. Cache is gated to `not training and not torch.compiler.is_compiling()`, so the compiled sample graph traces the unchanged path (precomputed_q stays None) and is byte-identical. Output-preserving: precomputed_q equals the recomputed normed+projected query exactly (frozen weights, identical input values; expand makes all batch rows identical). Mechanism: collapses many redundant RMSNorm/GEMM launches per predict in the launch-bound regime; neutral on sample.

Big-lever, output-preserving CUDA-graph capture of the stage-3 ICL block loop on the EAGER (typical, <8192-row) path. This model is launch-bound (GPU 25-60% busy); the ICL loop issues a storm of tiny kernels per block (RMSNorm pow/mean/rsqrt/mul, fused projections, flash-attn, GELU MLP) with the host on the critical path. torch.compile was rejected on typical (recompile thrash + Inductor fp reordering); a manual CUDA graph instead REPLAYS the exact same eager kernels in the exact same order on fresh data copied into a static input buffer — bit-identical output, zero fp reordering — collapsing thousands of launches into one graph replay. The block loop has no in-place mutation of its input on the full path (save_peak_memory_factor is None => chunked_evaluate returns x+f(x)), no RoPE, and reads only frozen/lazy-cached weights (fused-projection + base_scales caches are keyed stably and persist in module __dict__, so addresses are stable across replays). Mechanism: per (shape, num_train, dtype) key, warm up 3 iters on a side stream (populating SDPA/cublas workspaces + the frozen-weight/base-scales caches via the cache-HIT path so only reads are captured), then capture once and on every repeat copy_ fresh data into the static buffer, replay, and clone the output. Strictly gated to the eager regime (cuda, not training, grad disabled, not compiling, not enable_torch_compile so the accepted compiled sample path stays byte-identical, not force_recompute, save_peak_memory_factor is None). Robustness: capture wrapped in try/except with a None sentinel that falls back to the eager loop; an 8-graph cap bounds memory; _icl_graph_cache popped in __getstate__ (CUDAGraph unpicklable). Expected: large launch-overhead reduction on the launch-bound typical point, bit-identical outputs, neutral on sample.

Every TabPFNV3 attention call routes through the shared `scaled_dot_product_attention`, which wraps each F.sdpa dispatch in `with sdpa_kernel(backends=_SDPA_BACKENDS)`. That context manager sets and then restores the global SDPA backend-priority flags on EVERY call (hundreds per predict: ~12 ICL blocks + dist-embedder + column-aggregator + decoder), all with the identical constant backend list — pure host-side critical-path overhead in this launch/host-bound regime (GPU only 25-60% busy). Since the list is constant, entering that context once process-wide and keeping it active is exactly equivalent to re-entering it per call: the SAME backends remain active, so the chosen kernel and the math/output are bit-identical. We activate the default context exactly once (lazily) and then run the dispatch loop without re-entering it, removing the per-call enter/exit (flag set + restore + C++ dispatch) across all attention calls. The rare `_backends_override` path (tests only) still uses a nested temporary context, so it is unaffected. Output-preserving: identical backend selection => identical SDPA kernel => bit-identical results; only redundant host-side context-manager churn is eliminated, trimming the host critical path on both operating points.

In ColumnAggregator's self-attention (Attention.forward), RoPE is applied to q and k via two SEPARATE rotate_queries_or_keys calls per layer. Each independently rebuilds the identical, data-independent cos/sin chain (arange + outer-mul + cos + sin + 2×cat ≈ 6 small kernels) for the SAME seq_len — so it runs 2× per layer per row-chunk per predict in the launch-bound eager regime. Add apply_rope_pair / rotate_queries_and_keys that computes positions/freqs/cos/sin ONCE and applies them to both q and k, halving those redundant small kernel launches per layer. This is materially different from the previously-rejected cross-call cos/sin dict-cache (no-speedup 1.0183, but typical was already 1.0335): it is pure within-call sharing with ZERO python/lookup overhead and NO extra data movement of the big q/k tensors — the per-tensor rotate+combine stay byte-identical, only the duplicate cos/sin build is elided. Bit-identical: q and k each rotated by the exact same ops on the exact same cos/sin values, combine cast uses each tensor's own dtype as before; q and k share seq_len in self-attention so cos/sin are genuinely identical. The compiled (sample) path traces the same fused expression (no python side effects), so it stays neutral/faster. Mechanism: fewer kernel launches in the launch-bound eager typical path.

In Attention.forward (ColumnAggregator self-attention), RoPE is applied to q and k in two separate apply_rope calls. Each call independently recomputes the position/cos/sin chain (arange + outer-mul + cos + sin + 2×cat ≈ 6 kernels) and runs its own `t*cos + t_rotated*sin` combine — so per block the launch-bound eager (typical) path pays ~2× this storm even though q and k share the same seq_len, head_dim and frozen inv_freq. Concatenate q and k along the head axis into one (B,S,2H,D) tensor, apply RoPE ONCE, then split. This computes cos/sin a single time and issues one combine kernel (over twice the heads) instead of two, halving the RoPE-related kernel launches per aggregator block. Output-preserving/bit-exact: apply_rope rotates each position's head-dim vector independently of the head index, so stacking q's and k's heads and rotating together yields exactly the same per-head result as rotating them separately (same cos/sin per position, same fp ops, no reordering of the per-element math). Only applies when rope is not None (ColumnAggregator); other attention paths untouched. Expected: fewer tiny kernel launches on the launch-bound typical path, neutral on sample.

Compile the WHOLE stage-3 ICL block stack as ONE graph instead of per-block. The ICL loop runs ~nlayers ICLTransformerBlocks; trunk runs them eager even on the compile-enabled (>=8192-row, 'sample') path, and the prior per-block compile reached only 1.0309 (under the 3% bar) because each block paid a separate dynamo dispatch/guard boundary and Inductor could not fuse block i's MLP residual-add with block i+1's layernorm. Looping inside a single compiled method (_run_icl_blocks_compiled) lets torch.compile fuse the inter-block residual adds and the per-layer RMSNorm (aten::mean/pow/rsqrt ~398 each)/projection/softmax-scaling/MLP launch storm into one graph, eliminating ~(nlayers-1) per-block compiled-call dispatch boundaries in this launch-bound (GPU 25-60% busy) regime. num_train stays a python int (static guard, fixed per operating point so the train-key slice point is static); x_BRiD batch/row dims are mark_dynamic so varying test-row counts don't recompile. Strictly gated to the default predict path (enable_torch_compile AND not force_recompute_layer AND save_peak_memory_factor is None) — the eager small-input ('typical') path and all kv-cache / return_kv / checkpoint / chunked paths are byte-identical and untouched. Output-preserving: same math, only op-fusion/fp-reordering the gate already tolerates on the compiled sample path (which is above the GPU-nondeterminism floor). The compiled callable is cached via the existing _compiled() (popped in __getstate__ for picklability). Expected: pushes the near-miss ICL-compile gain over the 3% CI threshold on sample; neutral typical.

FA3 (Hopper) branch of the shared SDPA helper — the attention backend preferred on the H100 `sample` operating point (bf16, long seq) — still calls a BLANKET q.contiguous()/k.contiguous()/v.contiguous() before flash_attn_func, forcing full-tensor copies (aten::copy_ is the #1 GPU op). This is the one flash-feeding path NOT covered by the previously-accepted SDPA `.contiguous()` removal (+8.9%), which established that flash backends only require last-dim stride-1 contiguity. q_BSHD/k/v here are (B,S,H,D) projection / RoPE-output views whose head (last) dim is already stride-1 (RoPE's transpose-back keeps last-dim stride 1; projection outputs are fully contiguous). Replace the blanket copies with a last-dim-only guard `_ld_contig`: returns the tensor unchanged when stride(-1)==1 (eliminating the redundant full copy of strided RoPE outputs and the no-op copy of already-contiguous projection outputs), and only materialises a copy otherwise — so FA3 always receives at least the same last-dim contiguity the SDPA path proved sufficient. Strict no-regression: an already-contiguous tensor's .contiguous() was a no-op anyway, and the fallback still copies when last-dim isn't stride-1. Output is bit-identical (flash math is independent of batch/head/seq strides). Mechanism: fewer/zero large copy kernels + bytes moved on every FA3 attention call → faster sample; typical (uses the PyTorch SDPA branch, untouched) stays neutral.

aten::copy_ is the #1 GPU op (7.97ms/1886 calls). FeatureDistributionEmbedder runs each InducedSelfAttentionBlock via its forward, which does `transpose(1,2).contiguous()` on the INPUT and `transpose(1,2).contiguous()` on the OUTPUT — between consecutive layers this is a wasteful round trip (block i materializes (B,Ri,C,E), block i+1 immediately re-folds to (B*C,Ri,E)). Materially different from the two prior single-side .contiguous() drops (which left a strided handoff and went neutral/regressed): fold the column axis into the batch dim ONCE around the whole stack, run all blocks directly on the (B*C,Ri,E) layout via _induced_attention, then unfold ONCE — removing 2*(num_layers-1) large contiguous copies per dist-embedder pass. Intermediates stay contiguous (each CrossAttentionBlock returns x+mlp_out, an elementwise add), so no strided-access slowdown. Bit-identical: transpose+reshape and its inverse are value-preserving and this exactly mirrors layer.forward when save_peak_memory_factor is None (chunked_evaluate with factor None is a passthrough with residual=False). Strictly scoped to the eager common path (not return_hidden, not force_recompute_layer, save_peak_memory_factor is None, not compiling); all other paths fall through to the unchanged per-block loop. const-q cache on cross_attn_block1 still fires. Mechanism: fewer + larger contiguous copies → fewer copy kernels in the launch/copy-bound typical regime.

RoPE cos/sin are data-independent (depend only on seq_len for this module's frozen inv_freq/interleaved), yet `apply_rope` rebuilds them (arange + outer-mul + cos + sin + 2×cat ≈ 6 small kernels) on EVERY rotate call — q and k in each ColumnAggregator block, every block sharing the same `self.rope`, and every timed predict at the same fixed seq_len. A prior memoization attempt got typical +3.35% mean but missed the >3% CI bar (lower 1.0212), and the likely culprit was its heavyweight key: a per-FUNCTION global dict keyed on `(id(inv_freq), inv_freq._version, seq_len, device, interleaved)` — a 5-tuple built (and a shared dict probed) on every call, adding python/launch overhead that inflated variance and depressed the mean below the bar. This is materially different: a PER-INSTANCE cache keyed only on the integer `seq_len` (single int compare + device check, no id()/._version tuple construction, no cross-instance dict), so the cache-hit path is essentially free and only the genuine cos/sin builds are elided. On hit, `_apply_rope_cached` reuses the already-doubled cos/sin and runs only the per-tensor combine (t_rotated cat + 2 mul + add + cast) — bit-identical to `apply_rope` (same cos/sin values, same fp ops, same final cast to t.dtype). Gated to `not training and not torch.compiler.is_compiling()`, so the compiled (sample) graph is unchanged with no python-side-effect graph break (sample neutral). Mechanism: removes ~6 tiny kernel launches per rotate call across all blocks/q/k/predicts in the launch-bound eager (typical) regime, with near-zero lookup overhead → should reproduce the +3.3% typical effect with tighter CI that clears the 3% bar.

Stage-3 ICL transformer blocks run EAGER even on the sample (>=8192-row) path where torch.compile is already enabled+proven for stages 0-2. Each ICLTransformerBlock issues a launch storm (RMSNorm pow/mean/add/rsqrt/mul, Q/KV projections, softmax-scaling pointwise, flash-attn, GELU MLP) as dozens of tiny eager kernels per layer per predict -- pure launch overhead in the 25-60%-GPU-busy regime. Extend the existing compile coverage to the ICL loop: compile each block once, keyed on id(block) (NOT method.__func__, which would wrongly alias the distinct-weight blocks that share one forward code object), with dynamic=True so varying test-row counts don't recompile. num_train stays a python-int positional arg (fixed per operating point) so the train-key slice point is static; save_peak_memory_factor is None on the sample full path and traced as a constant. This collapses the per-block eager launch storm into fused Inductor kernels -- the same mechanism that already sped up stages 0-2 (+8.6%). STRICTLY gated to performance_options.enable_torch_compile (False for <8192 rows, set at line 1883), so the eager 'typical' path is byte-identical and only the already-compile-tolerant 'sample' path is touched (fp-reordering within the argmax+JS gate's tolerance). The force_recompute_layer (checkpoint) and return_kv/cached_kv paths are left completely untouched. The compiled callables live in the existing self._torch_compile_cache dict, which __getstate__ already pops, so picklability is unaffected. Expected: large launch-overhead reduction on the sample point (~+3%), neutral/byte-identical typical.

SoftmaxScalingMLP.forward's elementwise scaling tail -- tanh, (1 + .), * base_scales, * q -- is ~3 separate full [B,S,H,D] pointwise kernels per scaled-attention call. It runs in every ICL block, every dist-embedder cross-attn block, and the decoder, so aten::mul (6.86ms/1181, the #2 GPU op) is dominated by it in the launch-bound eager (typical) regime where the GPU is 25-60% busy. Collapse the tail into ONE compiled triton kernel via a lazily-built, shared (dynamic=True) compiled helper. Because the tail is PURELY ELEMENTWISE, the fused kernel computes each output element with the identical op sequence q*(base_scales*(1+tanh(query_out))) the eager code already uses -- no reduction reordering -- so it is bit-identical and safe for the deterministic small-input gate. This is materially different from the previously-rejected SoftmaxScaling edits: those reassociated/addcmul'd the muls IN EAGER (regressed -- broadcast addcmul hit a non-fused path), whereas here we fuse via compile. Gated to small S (q_BSHD.shape[1] < 8192, mirroring the base_scales size-gate) so the large-input (sample) ICL path -- which runs these eager with large S -- never pays the per-call Dynamo dispatch, and skipped under torch.compiler.is_compiling()/training so the compiled sample graph and training are byte-identical. query_mlp's GEMMs stay eager; only the cheap elementwise epilogue is fused. Expected: fewer kernel launches per scaled-attention call on the launch-bound typical point, bit-identical outputs, neutral on sample.

RoPE cos/sin (arange + outer-mul + cos + sin + 2×cat ≈ 6 tiny kernels) are recomputed on every rotate_queries_or_keys call. ColumnAggregator shares ONE RotaryEmbedding across all of its self-attention blocks, invoking it for both q and k, so the SAME cos/sin (depending only on seq_len=num_cls+num_columns, device, and the frozen inv_freq — never on tensor values) are regenerated 2×num_layers times per predict, a pure launch storm in the launch-bound eager (typical) regime. Memoise the FINAL (S,D) cos/sin in a single slot keyed on (seq_len, device) — a cheap scalar compare, NOT the expensive per-key dict that made the earlier RoPE cache regress (1.0112). This is the exact single-slot pattern that turned the rejected dict-based base_scales cache into an accepted win. apply_rope gains optional precomputed cos/sin args; when supplied it skips the arange/cos/sin/cat chain and runs only the value-dependent t_rotated + combine, so the result is bit-identical (same fp32 cos/sin values, same `(t*cos + t_rotated*sin).to(dtype)` arithmetic, same order). Gated to `not training and isinstance(seq_len,int) and seq_len<8192 and not torch.compiler.is_compiling()`, so the compiled sample-path graph (ColumnAggregator runs inside the compiled _process_row_chunk) traces the unchanged inline path and stays byte-identical/neutral, while the eager typical path collapses all redundant cos/sin builds to one per distinct seq_len. Cached tensors are plain (picklable), so no __getstate__ change is needed. Expected: fewer kernel launches → faster typical, neutral sample.

aten::copy_ is the #1 GPU op (cast/copy heavy). The cached fused projection weight (used by ICL kv, ColumnAggregator qkv, CrossAttention kv) is stored in its source dtype (fp32). Under autocast, F.linear casts this full fused weight to the autocast dtype on every call — a redundant full-tensor weight copy per attention call. Pre-cast the fused weight ONCE to the currently-active autocast dtype and reuse it (keyed in a per-cache-entry sub-dict), so autocast's per-call weight cast becomes a no-op (weight already in target dtype). Bit-identical: autocast would have produced the exact same lower-precision weight; the input is still autocast-cast as before, so the matmul math is unchanged. Strictly guarded: only when autocast is enabled for the weight's device type and the dtypes differ; any introspection failure falls back to the original fused weight (neutral). Skipped during training/compile (returns None as before). Expected: removes ~one full weight-cast copy per fused-projection call across ICL/column/cross blocks per predict in the launch/copy-bound regime, neutral when not under autocast.

Extend the ACCEPTED constant-query cache in CrossAttentionBlock (dist-embedder cross_attn_block1) to also fold the softmax-scaling step into the cached query. Today the cache stores only the PROJECTED query; SDPA then still runs the full SoftmaxScalingMLP on that query on every call: query_mlp (2 Linear GEMMs + GELU) + tanh + (1+.) + base*mod + q*scales = ~6 tiny eager kernels per dist-embedder block per predict, on top of the cached const-q win. This is bit-exact-foldable because: (1) cross_attn_block1's query is the frozen `inducing_vectors` expanded over the batch (shares storage -> values identical every call), already cached keyed on its storage ptr; (2) SoftmaxScalingMLP.forward(q_BSHD, n) (lines 657-711) computes q*base_scales(n)*(1+tanh(query_mlp(q))) -- base_scales depends ONLY on n, modulation depends ONLY on q, so the scaled query is a pure deterministic function of (constant q, n) with frozen inference weights; (3) n = src_len = k.shape[1] = V = context length, which I now fold into the cache key; (4) the scaling is applied at EXACTLY the same point and shape as in SDPA -- _batched_scaled_dot_product_attention applies softmax_scaling_layer(q_BSHD, k.shape[1]) to q in head shape (B,S,H,D), and precomputed_q is already that exact (B,Q,H,D) head-shaped projected query, so there is no reshape/commute concern. I pre-scale precomputed_q when populating the cache and pass a new `precomputed_q_is_scaled` flag so CrossAttention bypasses re-scaling (passes softmax_scaling_layer=None into SDPA). The flag is `precomputed_q is not None and self.attn.softmax_scaling_layer is not None`, consistent on both cache hit (cached value is already scaled) and miss (just scaled). Strictly gated to `not training and not torch.compiler.is_compiling()` (unchanged), so the compiled sample stages-0-2 graph traces the original path (precomputed_q stays None, flag False) and is byte-identical; cross_attn_block2 (non-constant query, no scaling layer) is unaffected. Mechanism: collapses ~6 redundant eager kernel launches per dist-embedder block per predict in the launch-bound typical regime, on top of the const-q/base_scales/fused-QKV wins; neutral on sample.

In TransformerBlock.forward_cross (ColumnAggregator's CLS-readout block), the query (cls_part = x_full[..., :num_cls, :]) is exactly the leading-Q slice of the context (x_full). RMSNorm normalizes each (...,E) vector independently of the others, so layernorm(query_BRQE) is bit-identical to layernorm(context_BRCE)[..., :Q, :]. Currently we issue TWO separate RMSNorm launch-storms (pow/mean/add/rsqrt/mul) — one on the query, one on the full context — then run k/v as two separate GEMMs. Compute the context norm ONCE and slice the query norm out of it (eliminating one full RMSNorm), and fuse the K/V projections (identical input c_flat) into a single cached F.linear via the existing _fused_projection_weight helper (cutting one GEMM launch). Output-preserving: same RMSNorm math reused on identical values, fused-then-sliced K/V equals the two separate projections exactly (frozen weights), and the fused-weight cache is skipped under training/compile so the compiled sample path is byte-identical. Mechanism: fewer kernel launches in the launch-bound eager regime.

forward_cross (ColumnAggregator CLS-readout, last block) RMSNorms the query and the context separately. Its sole non-checkpoint caller passes query = context[..., :num_cls, :] (the literal prefix slice cls_part of x_full). RMSNorm acts independently per token over the last dim E, so layernorm(query) == layernorm(context)[..., :Q, :] bit-for-bit. I add an explicit `query_is_context_prefix` flag (set True only by that provably-prefix caller) that normalizes the context ONCE and reuses its prefix for the query, eliminating one redundant RMSNorm (the mean/pow/rsqrt/mul launch storm, ~5 tiny kernels) per predict on the launch-bound path. Output-preserving (identical values, just a deleted duplicate norm); the force_recompute/checkpoint branch keeps the original two-norm path so nothing else changes. Mechanism: fewer kernel launches in the GPU-underutilized regime.

In Attention.forward (ColumnAggregator self-attention, run per block on the launch-bound typical eager path), RoPE is applied to q and k via two independent apply_rope calls. q and k ALWAYS have the same sequence length in self-attention, so each call recomputes the bit-identical cos/sin chain (arange→outer-mul→cos→sin→2×cat ≈ 6 tiny kernels) redundantly. Add apply_rope_pair / RotaryEmbedding.rotate_q_and_k that computes positions/cos/sin ONCE and rotates both q and k, halving the cos/sin launch count per self-attention call. Output-preserving: identical arithmetic and dtype handling, same cos/sin values (same seq_len, same inv_freq), only computed once instead of twice — no caching, no dict lookup, no fp-combine fusion (distinct from prior rejected RoPE ideas). Only applied where q/k share seq_len (Attention.forward); forward_cross with differing Q/V lengths is untouched. Mechanism: fewer tiny kernel launches in the launch-overhead-bound regime.

ManyClassDecoder.forward materializes one_hot_targets_BNHT via unsqueeze(2).expand(-1,-1,num_heads,-1).contiguous() — a full-size copy of the head-replicated (B,N,H,T) one-hot value tensor (H× larger than the source). It is passed straight to _chunked_class_attention as v_BJHT, which re-materializes contiguity regardless: F.pad (when pad>0 returns a fresh contiguous tensor) followed by reshape(B,J,H,num_chunks,D).permute(...).reshape(B*num_chunks,J,H,D).contiguous(). The class axis (T) of one_hot_targets_BNT is already contiguous (stride 1, from the .to().contiguous() above), so splitting T→(num_chunks,D) on the H-stride-0 expanded view is a valid view, and the final reshape's .contiguous() supplies the single needed copy. Thus the explicit .contiguous() on the expand is a redundant large copy in the launch/copy-bound regime (aten::copy_ is the #1 GPU op). Dropping it removes one full H-expanded copy per predict (largest for the sample point with many train rows × classes). Output-preserving: only contiguity/layout differs; all values identical — same proven pattern as the accepted SDPA-q/k/v .contiguous() removal.

_DtypeMatchingRMSNorm.forward issues self.weight.to(input.dtype) on every call (~398/predict under autocast: fp32 weight, fp16 input). Each is a tiny copy KERNEL LAUNCH — and aten::copy_ is the #1 GPU op (1886 calls, ~4µs each = launch overhead) in the launch-bound typical/eager regime. The previously-rejected weight-cache keyed on a (data_ptr, weight._version, dtype) tuple built+compared every call; that python cost exceeded the cheap tiny copy, so it was ~1.4% slower. This version is MATERIALLY LEANER: it stores the casted tensor directly in __dict__ and only does one dict.get + two attribute compares (dtype/device) — strictly cheaper than the ~5µs kernel launch it replaces. In inference the weight is frozen, so the cached casted tensor is bit-identical to a fresh .to() (same values, same F.rms_norm call) → outputs unchanged. Gated to `not self.training and not torch.compiler.is_compiling()` so the compiled sample-path graph traces the unchanged plain .to() branch (no python-side-effect graph break, byte-identical). Mechanism: removes ~398 copy-kernel launches/predict in the launch-bound typical path; neutral on sample.

The accepted fused-projection cache stores the concatenated QKV/KV weight in fp32. The whole forward runs under torch.autocast, and under torch.inference_mode autocast does NOT cache its weight casts (caching requires leaf+requires_grad), so every F.linear(x, fused_w) re-casts the LARGE fp32 fused weight to the fp16/bf16 compute dtype — a fresh aten::copy_ per call (copy_ is the #1 GPU op, 1886 calls/7.97ms, launch/copy-bound). These fused projections fire per ICL block, per column-aggregator block, and per dist-embedder cross-attn block (per column chunk in the eager 'typical' path).

Fix: when autocast is enabled for the weight's device, pre-cast the cached fused weight to the autocast compute dtype ONCE and reuse it, keyed on (versions, source dtype, target dtype, device). F.linear then sees a weight already in the compute dtype, so autocast performs no weight cast — removing the per-call large copy. The GEMM is bit-identical: autocast's per-call weight cast is exactly the same `.to(compute_dtype)` we now do once (same rounding), and the input is cast to the compute dtype identically either way (fp16@fp16, fp32 accumulate). Strictly gated: only triggers when autocast is enabled and the source weight is fp32; forced-fp16/fp32 (non-autocast) paths and the compiled/training paths keep the original fp32 fused weight unchanged. Expected: fewer + smaller copy_ launches on every fused projection, helping both operating points, neutral where autocast is off.

In Attention.forward (ColumnAggregator self-attention), RoPE is applied independently to q and k via two apply_rope calls, each rebuilding the cos/sin tables (arange + outer-mul + cos + sin + cat). Since q and k are self-attention tensors with identical seq_len/device/dtype and share the same frozen inv_freq, the cos/sin tables are bit-identical between the two calls. Factor apply_rope into a table-builder (_rope_cos_sin) + applier (_apply_rope_cos_sin), and add RotaryEmbedding.rotate_q_and_k that builds the tables ONCE and applies to both q and k. This removes ~5 tiny per-call kernels (the trig/cat chain) per self-attention block — pure launch overhead in this launch-bound regime — and is bit-identical (same fp32 intermediate math, same cast-back) to the prior two-call path. Distinct from the rejected cross-call cos/sin cache: no cross-call state, just intra-forward sharing, so no compile-thrash or cache-key risk.

Stage-3 ICL blocks run eager even on the large-input (sample) path where torch.compile is already enabled — 12 blocks × (RMSNorm storm + projections + SDPA + softmax-scaling + MLP) = a per-block eager launch storm, pure CPU launch overhead in this 25–60%-GPU-busy regime. Prior ICL attempts all stopped short: default-mode per-block compile hit 1.0309 (sub-CI), default-mode whole-loop regressed (1.0068), and a hand-rolled CUDA-graph capture regressed (0.9889, fragile buffer handling). This proposal uses the supported path: wrap the *entire* ICL loop in one torch.compile(mode="reduce-overhead") graph (CUDA graphs) with dynamic=False, so after a one-time per-shape capture the whole 12-block region replays as a single CUDA graph with ~zero CPU launch overhead. The large operating point keeps a stable shape across the repeated predicts, so it captures once then replays. Strictly scoped to enable_torch_compile AND save_peak_memory_factor is None AND not force_recompute AND not the KV-cache/return_kv paths — every other path (the eager small 'typical' path, chunking, checkpointing, caching) falls through to the byte-identical eager loop. Output-preserving: _run_icl_blocks runs the exact same blocks with the same num_train and save_peak_memory_factor=None (functional passthrough in chunked_evaluate); under is_compiling() the blocks trace their unfused-projection branch (math-identical). Only op-fusion/fp-reordering the gate already tolerates for the compiled sample path. Compile cache excluded from pickling via the existing __getstate__. Expected: large/sample sharply faster (launch overhead removed), typical untouched.

Collapse the RMSNorm launch-storm into ONE Inductor triton kernel per norm call. torch<2.12's eager F.rms_norm decomposes into ~5 full-tensor kernels (pow/mean/add/rsqrt/mul) — the profiler's aten::mean x400 (2.07ms) + aten::pow x403 (1.66ms) + aten::rsqrt x398 (0.63ms) + a big mul slice are essentially ALL from the ~400 _DtypeMatchingRMSNorm calls/predict. Compile F.rms_norm ONCE (dynamic=True, shared across every norm instance) so it fuses into a single kernel: ~2N-read+N-write instead of ~3N-read+2N-write, and ~1 launch instead of ~5 on the launch-bound path.

MATERIALLY DIFFERENT from the earlier bare compile-rms_norm attempt (NOTES 105/106, judged no-speedup at typical=1.0013/sample=1.0242): (1) trunk has since gained the accepted fused-QKV/KV projection, base_scales and const-q caches, which removed a large fraction of the OTHER per-call launches — so RMSNorm now occupies a much larger share of the residual launch-bound time, and removing its ~1600 tiny kernels should move the needle more on typical than it did then; (2) here the per-call weight dtype-cast is folded INSIDE the compiled region (passed as an arg, captured by Inductor) so the cast no longer issues a separate eager copy kernel on every affine norm.

Gated to `not training and not torch.compiler.is_compiling()`: under the compiled sample stages-0..2 (is_compiling True) the call returns plain F.rms_norm so the outer Inductor graph is byte-identical, while the eager ICL-loop / output norms (run eager even on the sample path) and the entire typical path get the fused kernel. Compile variants are bounded (≤2 normalized_shapes × weight-presence), well under cache_size_limit. Output-preserving: identical rms-norm math, only op-fusion / fp-reordering the argmax+JS gate already tolerates.

Apply the already-accepted weight-cast-cache pattern (iters 12/17/34: MLP._mlp_wcache, query_mlp, fused-QKV) to the only remaining un-cached Linear weights — ManyClassDecoder's q_projection and k_projection. Under torch.inference_mode()+autocast, autocast does NOT persist its fp32->bf16 weight/bias cast across calls, so each decoder forward re-casts both Linears' weight AND bias as redundant aten::copy_ (the #1 GPU op, 7.97ms/1886). The decoder runs once per multiclass predict — eagerly inside the typical whole-body CUDA graph (cast copies replayed every predict) and compiled on the sample path. Caching the cast params (frozen inference weights, version/dtype-keyed; tensors live permanently on the module so the captured graph's by-address reads stay valid) and calling F.linear directly elides those casts. Bit-identical: autocast would .to(q.dtype)-cast the same fp32 weight/bias (same round-to-nearest) and run the identical bf16 matmul; only the redundant per-call cast is removed. Gated to not-training / not-compiling and only when a cast is actually needed (weight.dtype != input dtype), so the compiled sample path (is_compiling True) and any forced-precision path are byte-identical. Mechanism: fewer copy kernels inside the launch/copy-bound decoder path on every predict.

RoPE cos/sin (arange + outer-mul + cos + sin + 2×cat ≈ 6 tiny kernels) are data-independent — they depend only on (seq_len, device) for the single shared ColumnAggregator `rope` instance, yet they are rebuilt on EVERY apply_rope call: for q AND k, in every TransformerBlock, plus forward_cross, across all column-aggregator layers, on the launch-bound eager (typical) path. Prior memoization attempts cached in a module-level dict keyed on (id(inv_freq), inv_freq._version, seq_len, device, interleaved); that per-call key construction + dict lookup added overhead that capped the win at typical mean 1.0335 / CI-lower 1.0212 (just under the 3% gate). This is a MATERIALLY DIFFERENT implementation: a lean per-instance cache (self._cos_sin_cache) keyed only on (int seq_len, device) — a single int+device compare, no id()/_version tuple, no dict — so the hot path pays essentially zero lookup overhead and the recomputed cos/sin builds collapse to one per distinct seq_len per predict (reused for q+k across all blocks AND across the harness's repeated timed predicts). apply_rope is refactored to accept optional precomputed cos/sin; only the cos/sin construction moves, the data-dependent t_rotated and the identical `(t*cos + t_rotated*sin).to(dtype)` arithmetic are unchanged → bit-identical values. Caching is skipped under torch.compiler.is_compiling() and training, so the compiled sample-path subgraphs are byte-identical (no graph break, no recompile) and the eager typical path gets fewer launches. Expected mechanism: fewer tiny kernel launches on the launch-bound typical point, neutral on sample.

forward_cross (ColumnAggregator's CLS-readout last block) is the ONLY attention site that still issues separate k_projection and v_projection GEMMs on the same input c_flat — every other self-attention site already fuses QKV/KV via the accepted _fused_projection_weight helper (iter that gave +1.02x). c_flat has shape (B*R, V, E) with R = all rows (rows folded into batch), so this is a sizeable batched GEMM run twice. Fuse k+v into a single F.linear over the cached concatenated weight, then slice — halving the projection launches and letting cuBLAS run one larger GEMM. Bit-identical: F.linear(c_flat, cat([Wk;Wv])) sliced equals the two separate projections (frozen inference weights, same input). The helper returns None under training/compile, so the compiled sample path traces the unchanged separate-projection code and is byte-identical; only the eager (typical) path uses the fused cached weight. Uses a distinct "_fused_kv_cache" attr on the Attention module, which never goes through Attention.forward (that path uses "_fused_qkv_cache"), so no key collision. Mechanism: fewer GEMM/launch in the launch-bound eager regime, neutral on sample.

In ManyClassDecoder's _chunked_class_attention, when num_chunks==1 (num_classes <= head_dim, the common multiclass case) the chunk-folding is mathematically an identity: q_folded/k_folded/v_folded are unsqueeze(1)+expand(...,1,...)+reshape+contiguous of a singleton chunk axis, and the output unfold is permute/reshape/slice over a size-1 axis. That whole machinery (≈10 reshape/expand/permute/contiguous ops + the trailing permute/reshape) issues a string of tiny kernels per predict in this launch-bound regime while producing a result bit-identical to a single SDPA call on the (already-padded) q,k,v followed by the [...,:T] trim. Add an early-return fast path for num_chunks==1 that dispatches one _batched_scaled_dot_product_attention directly and trims. Output-preserving (identical math, same SDPA backend, same scaling layer); only removes redundant view/copy ops. Mechanism: fewer kernel launches in the decoder, which runs once per predict on the largest retrieval tensors. Gated purely on num_chunks==1 so the multi-chunk path (many classes) is untouched.

In FeatureDistributionEmbedder.forward's eager fast path, the stack output is built as `x_flat.reshape(B,C,Ri,E).transpose(1,2).contiguous()`, allocating a full (B,Ri,C,E) copy (the largest tensor on the typical path). Its sole consumer is ColumnAggregator.forward, whose first op is `torch.cat((cls, x_BRiCE), dim=2)` — `cat` always materializes a new contiguous output and accepts strided inputs, so it re-copies the data anyway. The explicit `.contiguous()` is therefore redundant: removing it deletes one large aten::copy_ (the #1 GPU op) plus its kernel launch per predict, while the bytes that reach the model are bit-identical (cat's output is independent of input contiguity). Output-preserving (no math change, identical cat result); the per-block (non-fast) path and all other consumers are untouched. Expected mechanism: one fewer big copy + launch on the launch-bound eager path; cat reading a transposed view instead of a contiguous one is at worst neutral.

Stage-3 (the ICL block loop) and the final output_norm run EAGER on both operating points (stages 0-2 are already compiled). Each of the ~12 ICLTransformerBlocks issues the RMSNorm launch-storm (pow/mean/add/rsqrt/mul ~398 each), fused QKV/KV projections, softmax-scaling pointwise and MLP as dozens of tiny eager kernels — pure launch overhead in the 25-60%-GPU-busy regime. Compile each ICL block once (keyed on id(module) so the 12 weight-distinct blocks don't alias) with dynamic=True + mark_dynamic on batch/row dims (num_train stays a python-int static guard, so the train-key slice point is fixed), AND additionally compile the previously-eager final output_norm so its RMSNorm decomposition fuses into a single Inductor kernel. This is materially different from the prior per-block-only attempt (1.0309, just under the bar): folding the eager output_norm tail into the compiled surface adds fusion the per-block-only version couldn't reach, while avoiding the whole-loop ONE-graph mega-graph that regressed (num_train recompile / graph-size). Strictly gated to enable_torch_compile and not force_recompute_layer; the force_recompute checkpoint path, the KV-cache (cached_kv) path and the return_kv path are untouched and byte-identical. Output-preserving: identical math, only op-fusion/fp-reordering the gate already tolerates for the compiled stages-0-2. The compile cache is popped in __getstate__ for picklability like the existing one. Expected mechanism: collapses the per-block + output_norm launch storm into fused kernels, pushing the near-miss ICL-compile gain over the 3% CI threshold.

The dist-embedder's cross_attn_block1 already caches its constant projected query (frozen inducing_vectors) via _const_q_cache (prior accepted +6% on typical). But that block ALSO has a softmax_scaling_layer, so on every call CrossAttention re-runs SoftmaxScalingMLP on the constant query: query_mlp (Linear→GELU→Linear, a real GEMM over B*C×inducing rows) + tanh + base_scales*mod + q*scales. For a constant query with a fixed train-row source length (single_eval_pos, a data-SHAPE not data-VALUE → allowed), this scaled query is bit-identical every call/predict. Cache it (_const_scaled_q_cache keyed on the cached unscaled-q storage ptr + src_len) and pass q_already_scaled=True so CrossAttention calls scaled_dot_product_attention directly, skipping the scaling MLP. Bit-exact (same scaling math, applied once and reused; SDPA path identical to _batched with the scaled q). Gated to not-training/not-compiling like the existing const-q cache, so the compiled sample graph traces the unchanged path (q_already_scaled stays False). Mechanism: removes a per-block GEMM + ~4 pointwise launches per predict on the launch-bound eager typical path; neutral on sample.

Launch-bound CPU overhead reduction: every attention call routes through scaled_dot_product_attention, which enters a per-call `with sdpa_kernel(backends=_SDPA_BACKENDS)` context. That context manager pushes/pops several global SDP backend flags (multiple pybind calls) on EVERY one of the ~166 attention calls/predict -- pure host-side overhead in the launch-bound (GPU 25-60% busy) eager regime, and it never shows up in the GPU profiler. I hoist it: a new `sdpa_backend_context()` sets the default backend set ONCE for the whole TabPFNV3.forward compute region (stages 0-2 + ICL loop), and the inner per-call context is SKIPPED when (a) no per-call backend override is given (true for all v3 calls), (b) we're inside the hoisted context, and (c) not torch.compiler.is_compiling() (so the compiled sample-path graph traces exactly as before). Output-preserving: while skipping, the globally-active backend priority is byte-identical to the per-call set (_SDPA_BACKENDS), so the SAME kernel is selected -> identical math. Other architectures (v2_*) and the cache/decoder paths are untouched (flag defaults False -> they keep their own per-call context). Expected mechanism: removes ~165 context enter/exit flag-set storms per predict from the host critical path -> faster typical, neutral sample.

In FeatureDistributionEmbedder.forward's eager fast path, the stack output is built as `x_flat.reshape(B, C, Ri, E).transpose(1, 2).contiguous()`. That trailing `.contiguous()` is a redundant full-size copy: the only consumer of this tensor is ColumnAggregator.forward, whose first operation is `torch.cat((cls, x_BRiCE), dim=2)`, and torch.cat always allocates a new contiguous output and copies its inputs (strided or not) — so the data is re-materialized regardless. Dropping the `.contiguous()` hands cat the strided (B, Ri, C, E) transpose view directly, eliminating one large aten::copy_ kernel per predict (copy is the #1 GPU op, 7.97ms/1886 calls; GPU only 25-60% busy in this launch/copy-bound regime). This is the SINGLE fast-path output copy (one per predict), materially distinct from the rejected per-block contiguous removal in the non-fast path (NOTES 85). Output-preserving: only the physical layout of the handoff tensor changes; cat produces a bit-identical contiguous result. Gated to the eager path (the fast path already requires `not torch.compiler.is_compiling()`), so the compiled sample subgraphs are byte-identical. Expected: fewer copy kernels + bytes moved on the typical point, neutral on sample.

The sample-path ICL loop (nlayers=24 ICLTransformerBlocks) runs EAGER even though stages 0-2 are compiled (+8.6% proven). Each block issues the RMSNorm launch-storm (pow/mean/add/rsqrt/mul), Q/K/V projections, softmax-scaling pointwise, and MLP as dozens of tiny eager kernels — pure launch overhead in this 25-60%-GPU-busy regime. Two prior compile granularities narrowly failed: PER-BLOCK (1.0309, CI lower 1.0286 < 1.03 — paid a dynamo dispatch/guard boundary per block, couldn't fuse block i's MLP residual-add with block i+1's layernorm) and the WHOLE loop as one graph (regressed — recompile/guard fragility, dynamic-shape thrash). This proposes a MIDDLE granularity never tried: compile the loop in GROUPS of 6 blocks via a single `_run_icl_group` bound method reused through the existing `_compiled` infra. This eliminates ~5/6 of the per-block dispatch boundaries (4 compiled calls instead of 24) and lets Inductor fuse each group's inter-block residual-add + next-block RMSNorm storm, while keeping the graph small enough to avoid the whole-loop's recompile fragility. start/end are python ints (dynamo specializes → loop unrolls, bounded variants); num_train stays a python-int static guard; x_BRiD batch/row dims are mark_dynamic so varying test-row counts don't recompile. STRICTLY gated to enable_torch_compile (False for <8192 rows), so the eager 'typical' path takes the unchanged per-block else-branch and is byte-identical; only the already-compile-tolerant 'sample' path is touched (fp-reordering within the gate's tolerance). force_recompute and return_kv / KV-cache paths are untouched. Output-preserving: identical block math, only op-fusion the gate already accepts for compiled subgraphs.

Inference runs under autocast (bf16) with fp32-stored weights, so PyTorch re-casts each Linear weight fp32->bf16 on EVERY call (autocast's weight-cast cache does not persist under inference_mode). The `MLP` (a plain nn.Sequential used in every ICL block ×24, every dist-embedder TransformerBlock, every CrossAttentionBlock) has its two LARGEST weights (emsize×dim_feedforward) re-cast each call — a sizeable share of aten::copy_, the #1 GPU op (7.97ms/1886) in this launch/copy-bound regime. Both Linears are bias=False. Override MLP.forward to cache the bf16-cast weights (frozen at inference; keyed on each weight's ._version + target dtype) and call F.linear directly, eliminating those redundant cast copies. Bit-identical: autocast would .to(input.dtype)-cast the same fp32 weights (same round-to-nearest) then run the identical bf16 matmul (fp32 accumulate) + GELU; we only elide the per-call re-cast. Gated to not-training/not-compiling and only when a cast is actually needed (weight.dtype != input.dtype), so the compiled sample path (is_compiling True; Inductor already fuses the cast) and any forced-precision fp32 path fall through to the original super().forward() and stay byte-identical. Expected mechanism: fewer/cheaper copy kernels on the eager typical path's hot MLP launch-storm.

Sample-path decoder un-compiled. On the sample path (enable_torch_compile=True, rows>=8192) stages 0-2 and the stage-3 ICL loop are torch.compiled, but the final ManyClassDecoder still runs fully EAGER on the large (B, M, E) test tensor: q/k projection GEMMs, an F.one_hot build, several full-tensor .contiguous() folds in _chunked_class_attention, flash SDPA, a head-mean, and a log/clamp — many separate bandwidth-bound launches over a large tensor in this launch/copy-bound regime. Route the decoder through the existing _compiled(dynamic=True) helper so Inductor fuses the pointwise/copy/reduction chain (one-hot cast + contiguous folds + mean + clamp/log) into far fewer kernels, the same mechanism that already sped up stages 0-2 and the ICL loop. mark_dynamic on the train/test row dims (index 1) keeps recompiles bounded across varying test/train counts; B and class count are fixed. Strictly scoped to enable_torch_compile AND not force_recompute_layer, so the typical operating point (compile disabled for <8192 rows → eager decoder) is byte-identical and cannot regress; only the already-compile-tolerant sample path changes, and only via Inductor op-fusion/fp-reordering the gate already tolerates there. Expected: sample faster (fewer launches + less in-graph memory traffic on the large decoder pass), typical unchanged.

aten::copy_ is the #1 GPU op (7.97ms/1886). Under torch.inference_mode()+autocast the per-call fp32->bf16 cast of frozen Linear weights is NOT memoized across calls, so every ICLAttention.forward re-casts q_projection.weight and out_projection.weight. These two projections run on every one of the ~24 ICL blocks and are NOT covered by the existing fused-KV cache (which only fuses k/v). This is exactly the pattern that was ACCEPTED for the MLP linears (typical=1.0627). Cache the cast weight per Linear (keyed on weight._version + dtype) and call F.linear directly, removing ~48 redundant cast copies/predict from the launch-bound eager (typical) path. Bit-identical: autocast performs the same .to(dtype) rounding then the same bias-free matmul; pre-casting just skips the no-op re-cast. Bypassed under training / torch.compile tracing (Inductor already fuses these casts on the compiled sample stages), and when weight.dtype already matches x.dtype. Expected: fewer copy kernels -> faster typical, neutral sample.

In TransformerBlock.forward_cross (ColumnAggregator's CLS-readout, last block, run once/predict on the launch-bound eager typical path), the query is normalized by self.layernorm separately from the context, but the only caller passes query == context[..., :Q, :] (cls_part is the leading prefix slice of x_full, sharing storage at offset 0). RMSNorm normalizes each (B,R,token) row independently over the last dim, so layernorm(context)[..., :Q, :] is BIT-IDENTICAL to layernorm(query). I compute the context norm once and slice its first Q tokens for the query, eliminating one redundant RMSNorm launch-storm (aten::pow/mean/add/rsqrt/mul — among the top GPU ops) per readout. A storage/shape guard preserves the general (non-prefix) case via the original independent norm, so the change is output-preserving for any usage. Mechanism: fewer kernel launches in the launch-bound regime; bit-exact (no fp reordering), so it satisfies the zero-floor typical gate.

aten::copy_ is the #1 GPU op (7.97ms/1886). FeatureDistributionEmbedder's documented, bit-identical fast path folds the column axis into the batch dim ONCE around the whole InducedSelfAttentionBlock stack instead of round-tripping transpose(1,2).contiguous() on the input AND output of every block. It is currently disabled under torch.compile (`not torch.compiler.is_compiling()`), so on the SAMPLE path — where `_process_row_chunk` (which calls this forward with save_peak_memory_factor=None, return_hidden=False, force_recompute=False) IS compiled — the traced graph contains the slow per-block path with 2·(nblocks−1) extra contiguity copies that Inductor must materialize. Removing the is_compiling gate lets the compiled graph trace the folded path, eliminating those per-block copy kernels from the captured sample graph. Output-preserving: the fast path is a documented bit-identical refactor (each block's elementwise x+mlp keeps the (B*C,Ri,E) handoff contiguous); only op count / fp-ordering inside the compiled graph changes, which the argmax+JS gate tolerates. The typical (eager) path is byte-identical since it already took the fast path (is_compiling False either way). Mechanism: fewer copy_ kernels + better fusion in the compiled sample graph → faster sample, neutral typical.

In ManyClassDecoder.forward, `one_hot_targets_BNHT = one_hot.unsqueeze(2).expand(-1,-1,H,-1).contiguous()` materializes the H-broadcast one-hot value tensor (a B*N*H*T copy — aten::copy_ is the #1 GPU op). This tensor is immediately passed to `_chunked_class_attention`, which independently re-lays it out via `v_BJHT.reshape(...).permute(...).reshape(...).contiguous()` — producing the actually-required contiguous layout. The expand's `.contiguous()` is therefore redundant: dropping it leaves a stride-0 broadcast view that the downstream reshape/F.pad path materializes exactly once (the reshape that splits T into (num_chunks,D) is a pure view of the contiguous T axis; the batch-merging reshape / trailing `.contiguous()` then does the single needed copy). Net: eliminate one large copy of the broadcast one-hot per predict (both operating points; especially large on the sample path where N is big), with no math/shape/precision change — only the physical layout of an intermediate is affected, values are bit-identical. Cannot be confidently slower since the number of materializing copies is strictly ≤ before.

RoPE cos/sin (arange + outer-mul + cos + sin + 2×cat) are recomputed on EVERY rotate_queries_or_keys call. ColumnAggregator shares ONE RotaryEmbedding across all self-attention blocks, invoking it for both q and k, so the SAME doubled (S,D) cos/sin — depending only on seq_len (=num_cls+num_columns), device, and the frozen inv_freq, never on tensor values — are regenerated 2×num_layers (+ the forward_cross block) times per predict, a launch storm in the launch-bound eager (typical) regime. Memoise the FINAL doubled cos/sin in a single per-instance slot keyed on (seq_len, device) — a cheap int+device compare, NOT the heavyweight per-key dict / id()/._version tuple that made earlier RoPE caches regress via variance — the exact single-slot pattern that turned the rejected base_scales cache into an accepted typical win. apply_rope gains optional precomputed cos/sin args; when supplied it skips the arange/cos/sin/cat chain and runs only the value-dependent t_rotated + combine, so the result is bit-identical (same fp32 cos/sin values, same `(t*cos + t_rotated*sin).to(dtype)` arithmetic, same op order). Gated to `not interleaved and not training and not torch.compiler.is_compiling() and seq_len<8192`: the sample (>=8192-row) path runs ColumnAggregator INSIDE the compiled _process_row_chunk, so is_compiling()=True bypasses the cache and traces the unchanged inline apply_rope (byte-identical compiled graph, no python-side-effect graph break) — this is why prior fork attempts regressed sample (their ColumnAggregator wasn't compiled) and this trunk stays neutral. Mechanism: removes ~6 tiny kernel launches per rotate call across all blocks/q/k/predicts in the launch-bound eager typical regime with near-zero lookup overhead.

Fold the pre-MLP RMSNorm affine weight into the MLP's first (bias-free) Linear in CrossAttentionBlock (the dist-embedder's per-column block, a hot launch-bound loop on the typical path). Currently `mlp(layernorm2(x))` runs `F.rms_norm` (normalize + a separate `* weight` elementwise kernel, part of the 1181-call/6.86ms aten::mul bucket) AND a per-call weight-cast copy (aten::copy_ is the #1 op), then mlp[0] matmul. Since the norm output feeds ONLY this Linear, `W0 @ (normalize(x) * w_norm) == (W0 ⊙_col w_norm) @ normalize(x)`. We precompute the folded weight `W0 * w_norm` once (cached on the module keyed on weight versions/dtype/device, exactly like the accepted `_fused_projection_weight` cache), then run `F.rms_norm(x, shape, None, eps)` (no weight mul, no weight cast) followed by `F.linear(normed, folded_W)`. This removes one elementwise mul kernel + one weight-cast copy per CrossAttentionBlock MLP call (many per predict: dist_embed_num_blocks × column-folded batch). Output-preserving up to fp reassociation of a per-channel scalar multiply, which the argmax+JS gate already tolerates for compile/fusion. Strictly gated to inference (not training, not torch.compile tracing) via the helper; the compiled/training path falls back to the original `mlp(layernorm2(x))` so graphs/grads are byte-identical. Expected mechanism: fewer tiny kernel launches + less bandwidth in the launch-bound dist-embedder loop.

forward_cross (ColumnAggregator's CLS-readout last block) is the ONLY attention path still issuing k and v as two SEPARATE matmuls over the same input c_flat -- the accepted fused-QKV/KV optimization (iter: +2.1%) was applied to Attention/CrossAttention/ICLAttention but never to this path. Fuse k_projection+v_projection into one F.linear over a cached concatenated weight (via the existing _fused_projection_weight helper, frozen-inference-only, version/dtype/device-keyed), then slice apart. This removes one GEMM launch plus its associated autocast fp32->bf16 weight-cast copy (aten::copy_ is the #1 GPU op) per predict in this launch/overhead-bound regime (GPU 25-60% busy). Bit-identical: both projections are bias=False so each fused output element is the same dot product as the separate projection; the (B*R,V,2,H,D) reshape preserves k/v ordering and last-dim (head_dim) stride 1 so the strided views stay flash-eligible. Gated via _fused_projection_weight to not-training/not-compiling, so the compiled sample path falls back to the identical separate-projection graph (returns None) and is byte-identical; only the eager typical path gets the fused weight. Expected: fewer GEMM + copy launches -> faster typical, neutral sample.

Sample-path ICL loop is compiled in groups (accepted mechanism, +3.5%). Currently group_size=6 → 24 blocks compiled as 4 separate graphs, i.e. 4 graph-dispatch/guard boundaries per predict with a full eager handoff (residual tensor materialize + Python re-entry) between each group. Raising group_size to 8 → 3 graphs instead of 4 removes one inter-group dispatch boundary and lets Inductor fuse one more group's worth of inter-block residual-add + RMSNorm/projection/MLP launch storm into a single graph. This is well short of the "one giant graph" extreme (which regressed via guard fragility), so guards/recompiles stay bounded (start/end stay python ints → unrolled; row/batch dims remain mark_dynamic). Sample path tolerates Inductor fp-reorder (within run-to-run noise); typical path is untouched (compile disabled for <8192 rows). Expected: fewer graph boundaries → faster sample, neutral typical.

apply_rope's non-interleaved branch builds two doubled (S,D) cos/sin tensors via `torch.cat((cos,cos))`/`torch.cat((sin,sin))` AND a full-width rotated copy `torch.cat((-t[half:], t[:half]))`, then does two full-width muls + an add + a cast — ~8 launches over full-width / negated / cat'd tensors, twice per ColumnAggregator block (q and k) and in forward_cross. Rotate the two contiguous halves directly instead: out1 = t1*cos - t2*sin, out2 = t2*cos + t1*sin, then one cat. This eliminates the two cos/sin doubling cats, the explicit negation, and the full-width rotate-cat, replacing them with half-width elementwise ops + one output cat — fewer/smaller kernel launches and less memory traffic in this launch-bound eager regime. It is mathematically bit-identical (the original t*cos + rotate(t)*sin expands per element to exactly t1*cos - t2*sin and t2*cos + t1*sin, cos/sin broadcasting over each half), so outputs are unchanged; the interleaved branch and the fp32-compute/cast-back behaviour are untouched. Materially different from prior RoPE attempts (cross-call cos/sin caching, addcmul fusion, q/k pairing): this restructures the rotation arithmetic itself to drop full-width cat/neg launches.

On the compiled (enable_torch_compile) path, stages 0-2 and the ICL block loop are compiled (ICL in groups of 6 — accepted), but the FINAL `output_norm` (a _DtypeMatchingRMSNorm over the full (B,R,D) hidden state) still runs EAGER after the loop, issuing its RMSNorm launch-storm (pow/mean/add/rsqrt/mul + weight cast) as separate tiny kernels and forming a dispatch boundary right after the last compiled group. Fold `output_norm` into the LAST compiled ICL group (`_run_icl_group` gets an `apply_output_norm` flag, set True only when `end == num_icl`) so Inductor fuses the last block's MLP residual-add with the output-norm reduction into the existing graph — collapsing those launches and removing the post-loop eager boundary. This is materially DIFFERENT from the rejected "whole ICL loop + output_norm as ONE giant graph" (regression 1.0068x, blamed on the single-graph guard/recompile fragility): I keep the accepted group_size=6 granularity and only append the norm to the already-distinct last-group graph, so no new compile variants are introduced (start/end are python-int constants → each group already its own specialization; apply_output_norm is constant-folded). Output-preserving: identical rms-norm math, only op-fusion/fp-reordering that the gate already tolerates on this compiled path. Strictly scoped to the compile branch (enable_torch_compile and not force_recompute and not return_kv_cache); the cache, return_kv, and eager-fallback paths still apply output_norm exactly once via the guarded post-loop call, so they are byte-identical. Expected: launch-overhead reduction on the compile path, neutral elsewhere.

The sample (>=8192-row) path is the only path that enters `_compiled()`, and it currently uses `torch.compile(method, dynamic=True)` with the DEFAULT Inductor mode. Default mode fuses pointwise ops but leaves every GEMM on stock cuBLAS with NO template autotuning and NO matmul epilogue fusion. The diagnostics show aten::mm (3.98ms/800) + aten::addmm (1.86ms/473) ≈ 5.8ms of GEMM, which dominates the compute-bound sample point (currently ~1.0x — lots of headroom).
